summary: Gruber challenges a Strategy Analytics report claiming Android had captured 30% of the tablet market, arguing that their 'shipment share' metric conflates shipping units to retailers with actual sales to customers. Using Google's own published data on Honeycomb adoption (0.9% of 135 million Android devices), he calculates only about 1.35 million Android tablets were actually in use, compared to Apple's 28.73 million iPads sold. He pushes back against the widespread assumption that Android will inevitably erode iPad dominance the way it did iPhone dominance in smartphones, arguing the tablet market is fundamentally different because Apple created it rather than entering an existing one. He draws a parallel to the iPod's sustained dominance of the music player market rather than the iPhone's trajectory in phones.
key_points: Strategy Analytics conflates unit shipments with actual sales, inflating Android's apparent tablet market position
Google's own data shows only ~1.35 million Honeycomb tablets in active use versus 28.73 million iPads sold — a 21:1 ratio
The assumption that Android will inevitably narrow the tablet gap mirrors flawed certainty, not analysis
The tablet market differs fundamentally from smartphones because Apple created it rather than entering an existing carrier-dominated market
The iPad's trajectory more closely resembles the iPod's decade-long dominance than the iPhone's smartphone competition
money_quotes: By their metric, a company could take the market share lead by shipping 10 million tablets and selling none of them to customers.
So it looks like Apple has sold, to customers, over 21 times more iPads than all Honeycomb Android tablets combined.

Not 'might not' or 'probably won't' but 'can't'. You don't have to look hard to find many other observers with the same belief — that Android will succeed in the tablet market similarly to how it has succeeded in the phone market. Why the certainty, though?
It reminds me of the certainty that many observers had, circa 2002-2005, that the iPod could not maintain a 70 percent share of the music player market.
The iPad didn't enter the tablet market. It created the tablet market.
Most people today still buy phones the same way they did in 2006: they go to their local mobile carrier store and buy whatever the sales staff there convinces them to buy.
There still really is no 'tablet' market — just an iPad market.
key_insight: The tablet market is not the smartphone market replayed — Apple created the tablet category with the iPad, making its dominance more analogous to the iPod's sustained leadership than the iPhone's eventually contested smartphone share.
